title: sign_verify_roundtrip test is its own oracle — violates test design constraint
description: key.rs tests::sign_verify_roundtrip calls key.sign(&hash) then key.verify(&hash, &sig) on the same TPM key. This proves the sign and verify paths are wired up but does NOT prove the signature is cryptographically correct — wolfTPM could return any bytes as 'sig' and then accept them as valid in verify. The CLAUDE.md test design constraint explicitly forbids this. A correct test would take a pre-computed ECDSA P-256 signature over the SHA-256 of 'hello' from an external source (e.g. OpenSSL) and verify that signature against the key's public component — or would export the public key and verify with a pure-Rust verifier. As written, the test is marked #[ignore] so it does not run in CI, but if it were ever enabled it would give false confidence.
